compiled_state.title: Government-specific UI/UX anti-pattern library for design review and planning
compiled_state.author: Claude
compiled_state.description:
Create a government-specific UI/UX anti-pattern library that agents consult during brainstorm, preplanning, implementation-plan, design review, and code review. This is modeled after ui-ux-pro-max-skill's 161 industry-specific reasoning rules, adapted for government digital services.

**Important:** The anti-patterns listed below are a starting point. During brainstorming for this epic, additional research on government-specific anti-patterns is required — consult USWDS documentation, 18F design principles, digital.gov guidance, Section 508 audit findings, and GSA/CISA compliance reports to expand and validate this catalog.

---

## Anti-Pattern Catalog (Initial Draft — Expand During Brainstorm)

### Authentication & Identity
1. **Smartphone-only auth** — Requiring a smartphone for MFA excludes users without one. Government services must offer alternatives (hardware tokens, phone call, in-person verification) per NIST 800-63B.
2. **Knowledge-based verification as primary auth** — Using security questions (mother's maiden name, first pet) as authentication. These are socially engineerable and exclude users with cognitive disabilities. Use phishing-resistant methods.
3. **CAPTCHA without accessible alternative** — Using visual/audio CAPTCHA that blocks screen reader users and users with cognitive disabilities. Use invisible CAPTCHA or alternative verification (WCAG 3.3.8).
4. **Session timeout without warning** — Sessions that expire silently, losing form data. Government forms can be lengthy; must warn before timeout and offer extension (WCAG 2.2.1 — minimum 20 minutes).
5. **Login.gov/ID.me-only without fallback** — Requiring a single identity provider with no alternative path for users who cannot complete digital identity proofing.

### Forms & Data Collection
6. **Asking for information the government already has** — Requesting SSN, address, or other data the agency already possesses. Violates Paperwork Reduction Act principles and WCAG 3.3.7 (Redundant Entry).
7. **Multi-page forms without save/resume** — Long government forms that cannot be saved and resumed later. Users with disabilities, unreliable internet, or time constraints lose all progress.
8. **Required fields without clear indication** — Using only color (red asterisk) to mark required fields. Violates WCAG 1.4.1 (Use of Color). Use text labels: "(required)".
9. **Date format ambiguity** — Accepting dates without specifying format (MM/DD/YYYY vs DD/MM/YYYY). Government forms serve international populations. Use date pickers with explicit format labels.
10. **Address fields assuming US format** — Rigid address forms that don't accommodate territories (PR, GU, AS, VI, MP), military addresses (APO/FPO), or tribal nations.
11. **Asking for gender/sex unnecessarily** — Collecting gender data when not required for the service. When required, limiting to binary options excludes non-binary individuals per current federal guidance.
12. **PDF-only form submission** — Requiring users to download, print, sign, and mail/scan PDFs. Excludes users without printers/scanners and is inaccessible to screen readers if not tagged.
13. **Auto-advancing multi-step forms** — Forms that automatically move to the next step without explicit user action. Disorienting for screen reader users and users with cognitive disabilities.

### Navigation & Information Architecture
14. **Jargon-based navigation labels** — Using agency acronyms or internal terminology in navigation (e.g., "SNAP Benefits" instead of "Food Assistance"). Users don't know your org chart.
15. **Search as the only discovery path** — Relying solely on search without browsable navigation. Users with cognitive disabilities and low digital literacy need structured paths.
16. **Mega-menus without keyboard support** — Complex dropdown menus that are mouse-only. All navigation must be fully keyboard-operable (WCAG 2.1.1).
17. **No breadcrumbs on deep pages** — Government sites often have deep hierarchies. Users arriving via search need orientation. USWDS includes breadcrumb component for this reason.
18. **Opening external links without warning** — Links to non-government sites that open without indicating the user is leaving a .gov domain. Users need to know they're leaving a trusted context.
19. **Breaking the back button** — Single-page apps or form wizards that don't preserve browser history, making the back button unpredictable.

### Content & Language
20. **Reading level above 8th grade** — Government content written at college reading level. Federal plain language guidelines require content accessible to the general public (plainlanguage.gov).
21. **Legal disclaimers as primary content** — Burying actionable information under walls of legal text. Lead with what the user needs to do; put legal notices in expandable sections.
22. **English-only critical services** — Providing essential government services only in English. Executive Order 13166 requires meaningful access for LEP (Limited English Proficiency) individuals.
23. **Acronyms without first-use expansion** — Using acronyms without expanding them on first use per page. Screen readers read acronyms as words, not initialisms, causing confusion.
24. **PDFs instead of web content** — Publishing information as downloadable PDFs instead of HTML pages. PDFs are harder to make accessible, don't work well on mobile, and can't be easily updated.

### Accessibility & Compliance
25. **Overlay widgets as compliance solution** — Using accessibility overlay tools (e.g., accessiBe, UserWay) instead of building accessible HTML. Overlays don't achieve compliance and can make experiences worse.
26. **Color-only status indicators** — Using only red/green to indicate form validation or application status. Fails WCAG 1.4.1. Use icons + text + color.
27. **Auto-playing video/audio** — Media that plays automatically on government pages. Violates WCAG 1.4.2, triggers vestibular issues, and is disruptive in public/shared spaces.
28. **Missing skip navigation** — No "Skip to main content" link. Government sites typically have heavy headers/navigation; keyboard users must tab through everything without it (WCAG 2.4.1).
29. **Images of text** — Using images containing text instead of styled HTML text. Cannot be resized, recolored, or read by screen readers (WCAG 1.4.5).
30. **Focus order doesn't match visual order** — Tab order that jumps around the page instead of following the visual flow. Disorienting for keyboard users (WCAG 2.4.3).
31. **Missing lang attribute** — HTML pages without a lang attribute. Screen readers default to the user's system language, mispronouncing all content if it doesn't match (WCAG 3.1.1).
32. **Decorative images with alt text** — Adding alt text to purely decorative images instead of using alt="". Screen readers announce irrelevant descriptions, cluttering the experience.

### Security & Privacy
33. **Third-party tracking without consent** — Embedding Google Analytics, Facebook pixels, or other trackers without user consent. Government sites are subject to OMB M-10-22 and FISMA.
34. **Exposing PII in URLs** — Passing SSN, case numbers, or other PII as URL query parameters. These appear in server logs, browser history, and referrer headers.
35. **Mixed content on .gov domains** — Loading HTTP resources on HTTPS .gov pages. Undermines trust and violates HSTS preloading requirements for .gov.
36. **Client-side-only input validation** — Validating form input only in JavaScript. Government forms handle sensitive data; validation must be server-side with client-side as enhancement only.

### Mobile & Responsive
37. **Desktop-only design** — Government sites that are not mobile-responsive. Over 60% of government site traffic is mobile. Connected America Act and OMB guidance require mobile support.
38. **Horizontal scrolling on mobile** — Content that requires horizontal scrolling on mobile viewports. Violates WCAG 1.4.10 (Reflow) and makes content inaccessible on small screens.
39. **Touch targets too small** — Interactive elements smaller than 44×44px. Government users include older adults and users with motor impairments who need adequate target sizes (WCAG 2.5.8).
40. **Fixed-position elements covering content** — Sticky headers/footers/chat widgets that obscure content on small screens, especially in landscape orientation.

### Performance & Infrastructure
41. **Heavy page weight assuming broadband** — Pages with large images, videos, and scripts assuming high-speed internet. Many government service users are in rural areas or on limited data plans.
42. **No offline/degraded mode for critical services** — Forms and applications that fail completely without JavaScript or on slow connections. Progressive enhancement is essential for government services.
43. **CDN dependencies for critical assets** — Loading fonts, frameworks, or icons from third-party CDNs. If the CDN goes down, the government service goes down. Self-host critical assets.

### Notifications & Status
44. **Email-only notifications for critical updates** — Notifying users only via email about benefit changes, deadlines, or required actions. Many government service users don't have reliable email access.
45. **No status tracking for submissions** — Users submit applications with no way to check processing status. Government processes can take weeks/months; users need visibility.
46. **Jargon in status messages** — Status updates using internal processing terms ("Your case is in adjudication") instead of plain language ("We're reviewing your application").

### Data Visualization
47. **Charts without data tables** — Visualizations without accessible data table alternatives. Screen reader users cannot access chart information (WCAG 1.1.1).
48. **Red/green-only encoding in maps and charts** — Using red/green to encode data categories, excluding users with color vision deficiency (~8% of males). Use patterns, labels, and shape in addition to color.

---

## Integration Points

1. **Brainstorm** — When brainstorming government UI epics, the anti-pattern library should be consulted during gap analysis. Additional research on domain-specific anti-patterns should be conducted for the specific government agency/program being served.
2. **Preplanning** — Accessibility and compliance reviewers should reference this catalog when generating cross-cutting concerns for UI stories.
3. **Implementation-plan** — Task acceptance criteria for UI work should include "does not introduce any government anti-patterns from the catalog."
4. **Design review** — Add government anti-pattern scan as a review dimension.
5. **Code review** — The accessibility overlay reviewer should check for automatable anti-patterns (missing lang attribute, images of text, color-only indicators, etc.).
6. **Completion verifier** — UI story closure should verify no anti-patterns were introduced.

## Open Work

This catalog is a starting point. The brainstorm phase for this epic must include additional research using:
- USWDS documentation and component guidance
- 18F Design Principles and Method Cards
- digital.gov web standards and policies
- GSA Section 508 test processes and common failure patterns
- DHS Trusted Tester conformance reports
- CISA web security guidance for .gov domains
- OMB Circular A-11 Section 280 (Managing Customer Experience)
- Executive Order 14058 (Transforming Federal Customer Experience)
